title: High-Protein Diet
whoThisIsFor: Active adults who struggle to reach protein targets using everyday foods.
explanation: Use this as a high-protein day template, not a fixed prescription. The meals are built around familiar foods so you can repeat the structure while changing the sabzi, dal, fruit, or grain based on what is available.
The main idea is to place a protein anchor in every meal. Besan, tofu, dal, sprouts, soy curd, and soy chunks do the heavy lifting; rice, roti, fruit, and vegetables make the day easier to eat.

detailSections.title: Adjust for taste and availability
items: If soy chunks are hard to enjoy, use tofu, tempeh, seitan, or an extra dal-and-sprouts meal instead.
Use any dal you digest well. Moong is lighter; chana dal, masoor, and toor are more filling.
Change the seasoning freely: tikka masala, sambar powder, pav bhaji masala, or simple cumin-turmeric all work as long as the protein food stays in the meal.

detailSections.title: Adjust portions
items: If body weight is not increasing and that is your goal, add one extra roti, more rice, or a peanut/banana snack.
If digestion feels heavy, split soy chunks or tofu across two meals instead of forcing a large serving at dinner.
If you need fewer calories, keep the protein portions and reduce oil, peanuts, rice, or roti first.

notes: Adjust portions based on body weight and goals.

title: Weight Loss Diet
whoThisIsFor: People who want a simple calorie-aware pattern without extreme restrictions.
explanation: This plan keeps the day filling by using legumes, soup-style meals, salad, and a planned snack instead of relying on willpower. The goal is a modest calorie deficit with enough protein to protect energy and training.
Treat the rice and roti portions as adjustable. The dal, rajma, chana, soy milk, and vegetables should stay consistent because they make the plan easier to follow.

detailSections.title: Adjust for hunger
items: If hunger is high, add more cucumber, tomato, cabbage, lauki, beans, or leafy vegetables before cutting calories further.
Keep roasted chana or fruit as the planned snack so evening hunger does not turn into fried snacks.
If dinner feels too light, make the moong dal thicker or add tofu cubes while keeping oil modest.

detailSections.title: Adjust for taste and routine
items: Rajma can be swapped with chole, kala chana, or masoor dal if that is what is cooked at home.
Tea or coffee is fine; the bigger difference is avoiding sugar-heavy biscuits and namkeen alongside it.
Use spices, lemon, coriander, pickle in small amounts, and chutneys to keep the plan satisfying.

notes: Keep the deficit moderate and monitor energy, hunger, and training recovery.

title: Muscle Gain Diet
whoThisIsFor: Lifters who need higher calories without relying only on shakes.
explanation: This is a higher-calorie template for people training hard and trying to gain weight slowly. The food volume can feel high, so the plan uses calorie-dense helpers like peanut butter, soy milk, rice, peanuts, and shakes.
Use the workout meals as flexible timing markers. If you train in the morning, move the fruit and peanuts before training and keep the shake or soy chunk meal afterward.

detailSections.title: Adjust for appetite
items: If you struggle to eat enough, use smoothies, oats, nut butters, banana, and rice because they are easier than very large salads.
If you feel too full, spread the same food across five smaller meals instead of three large meals.
If weight gain is too fast, reduce added fats first before reducing the main protein foods.

detailSections.title: Protein options
items: A plant protein shake is convenient, but it is not mandatory if soy chunks, tofu, tempeh, dal, and chana already cover your target.
Tempeh can be replaced with tofu, soy chunks, seitan, or extra chana depending on budget and availability.
Keep one reliable post-workout option ready so the plan does not depend on cooking immediately after training.

notes: Increase calories gradually if body weight is not moving.

title: Budget Diet
whoThisIsFor: Students and families prioritizing low-cost, repeatable meals.
explanation: This plan is built around low-cost staples that can be cooked in batches: besan, kala chana, masoor dal, rice, roti, and seasonal vegetables. It is intentionally simple so the same base can work across many weeks.
The plan becomes easier when legumes are soaked, boiled, and stored ahead. Then each meal is mostly assembly, reheating, and adding fresh vegetables or chutney.

detailSections.title: Adjust for budget and availability
items: Use the cheapest seasonal vegetable rather than chasing a specific sabzi.
Swap kala chana with rajma, chole, lobia, or whole moong based on local price.
If tofu or soy milk is outside budget, keep the plan legume-heavy and use peanuts or roasted chana for snacks.

detailSections.title: Batch-cooking notes
items: Soak and boil chana or rajma once, then use it for curry, salad, or chaat across two to three meals.
Cook extra dal and change the tadka or vegetables to avoid the meal feeling repetitive.
Keep chutney, lemon, onions, coriander, or pickle available so basic meals still taste complete.

notes: Batch cooking legumes makes the plan easier and cheaper.

title: Simple Beginner Plan
whoThisIsFor: Beginners who want a simple structure before tracking macros.
explanation: This is a starting pattern for people who want less decision-making. It gives you three familiar meals and enough structure to notice what keeps you full, energetic, and consistent.
Do not worry about perfect macros on day one. First make the meals repeatable, then adjust portions or protein foods once the routine feels normal.

detailSections.title: Easy swaps
items: Oats can become poha, upma, idli, or chilla if breakfast needs to be savory; add soy milk, dal, tofu, or sprouts elsewhere to keep protein up.
Tofu bhurji can be swapped with soy chunks, chana, rajma, or dal if tofu is not available.
Use any cooked vegetable sabzi at lunch; the habit matters more than the exact vegetable.

detailSections.title: How to progress
items: After one consistent week, increase protein first if meals feel light or hunger returns quickly.
If digestion is uncomfortable, increase legumes gradually and drink enough water.
If you miss a meal, return to the next planned meal instead of trying to compensate perfectly.

notes: Start with consistency, then adjust portions.

title: South Indian Plan
whoThisIsFor: People who prefer idli, dosa, rice, sambar, and familiar South Indian meals.
explanation: This plan keeps South Indian staples but nudges them toward more protein. Sambar, adai, sprouts, tofu poriyal, peanut chutney, and dal-heavy batters make the familiar pattern more filling.
Use the meal names as anchors. The exact vegetables, chutney, and spice level can change with season, household cooking, and personal taste.

detailSections.title: Adjust for local foods
items: Use idli, dosa, pesarattu, adai, or upma depending on what is available; pair lower-protein breakfasts with sambar or sprouts.
Tofu poriyal can be replaced with chana sundal, soy chunks, tempeh, or extra kootu.
Peanut chutney, podi, and sambar can make a simple meal more satisfying without needing expensive ingredients.

detailSections.title: Protein upgrades
items: Make sambar dal-heavy rather than watery when it is the main protein in the meal.
Use adai or pesarattu when dosa alone leaves you hungry.
Add sprouts sundal or roasted chana as a snack if the day is short on protein.

notes: Use dal-heavy batters and sambar to improve protein density.

title: North Indian Plan
goal: North Indian staples with protein focus
whoThisIsFor: People who prefer roti, dal, chana, rajma, and sabzi meals.
explanation: This plan keeps the North Indian meal shape: roti or rice with dal, rajma, chana, tofu, and sabzi. The upgrade is making the protein portion meaningful instead of treating dal as a thin side.
Use the plan as a weekly rotation. Rajma, chole, dal, tofu tikka, and besan cheela can rotate based on what is already cooked at home.

detailSections.title: Adjust for taste
items: Use the same masalas you already like for paneer, chole, rajma, or tikka; swap the protein base rather than changing the whole meal.
If tofu feels bland, press it, marinate it, and cook it on high heat before adding gravy.
If dal feels boring, change the tadka, add spinach, or use lemon and coriander at the end.

detailSections.title: Availability swaps
items: Rajma can be swapped with chole, lobia, kala chana, or whole moong.
Besan cheela can be swapped with moong dal cheela, tofu bhurji, or sprouts chaat.
Soy curd is optional; use peanut curd, fortified soy milk, or a non-curd side depending on what you can reliably buy.

notes: Keep oil moderate and make dal portions meaningful.

title: No Onion/No Garlic Plan
whoThisIsFor: People avoiding onion and garlic while keeping protein practical.
explanation: This plan avoids onion and garlic without reducing the day to plain starches. Protein still comes from moong dal, toor dal, peanut curd, tofu, and legumes.
Flavor has to come from other places: hing, cumin, ginger, tomato, curry leaves, coriander, lemon, coconut, sesame, and roasted spices.

detailSections.title: Flavor without onion or garlic
items: Use hing, ginger, cumin, mustard seeds, curry leaves, tomato, and coriander to build a base.
For richness, use peanut curd, sesame, coconut, cashew paste, or a small amount of ground peanuts.
Lemon juice or amchur at the end often makes no-onion/no-garlic dishes taste brighter.

detailSections.title: Protein swaps
items: Moong dal chilla can be swapped with besan chilla, adai, or sprouted moong chaat.
Tofu curry can be swapped with soy chunks, chana, rajma, or dal if tofu is unavailable.
Peanut curd can be replaced with soy curd or a peanut chutney bowl if fermentation is not practical.

notes: Use hing, ginger, cumin, and tomato for flavor without onion or garlic.
